content.problemStatement:
The government is surveying the entire planet in search of black money, and now there are **N** satellites orbiting Earth. Each satellite has sensors to detect black money transactions on the visible half of the planet. For example, when a satellite is passing over the north pole, its sensors can detect any transaction in the northern hemisphere. This surveillance has become a real pain point for some businessmen, as the satellite sensors are really good.

A powerful businessman wants to conduct a big black money transaction somewhere on the planet's surface. To do this, he must first sabotage all satellites that would be able to detect the transaction. The businessman can choose any place and time for the transaction, as long as it is on the planet's surface. Please help him by finding the minimum number of satellites that must be sabotaged so that the transaction goes undetected. He has promised you a small fraction of the money (in old currency) as a reward for your services.

The **ith** satellite orbits along longitude **li**, and at time **t=0**, is in phase **pi**. The phase of an orbiting body is the angle between the north pole, planet center, and the orbiting body. If phase **pi** is less than 180 degrees, the body lies on longitude **li** somewhere between the north pole and the south pole. If the phase is more than 180 degrees, the body lies on longitude **li-180** somewhere between the south pole and the north pole.

**Extra Explanation:** Note that the formulation of longitude and phase is slightly different from the system of longitudes and latitudes we use to denote points on Earth's surface. It is closer to the Spherical coordinate system, but we have constrained the azimuth angle (**li** in the figure above) to be between 0 and 180, so the azimuth angle of an orbiting satellite does not change, and the polar angle can be anywhere between 0 and 360. At time **t**, the **ith** satellite is located at:


**x = sin(pi + t) * cos(li)**
**y = sin(pi + t) * sin(li)**
**z = cos(pi + t)**
**Assumptions to be made:**


The transaction takes place in a place that has an arbitrarily small (but non-zero) area.
The transaction lasts for an arbitrarily short (but non-zero) duration.
All satellites have circular orbits that are very far away from the planet’s surface.
All satellites orbit at the same distance and the same speed.
All angles are in degrees.
The geometric center of the planet is the same as its center of mass.
No special or general relativistic effects.

Input Format
The first line of the input contains an integer **T** denoting the number of test cases. The description of **T** test cases follows.


The first line of each test case contains a single integer **N** denoting the number of satellites. The next **N** lines contain two space-separated integers each, **pi** and **li**.



Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing the minimum number of satellites that must be sabotaged to conduct a successful transaction.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The businessman can do the transaction at time t=0, at longitude 90, and phi 230 (90W, 40S), where no orbiting satellite can see them, so he does not need to sabotage any of them.
Example case 2: The businessman can do the transaction at time t=165 (after all satellites have completed 165 degrees of rotation), at longitude 121, and phi 269 (59W, 1S). No satellite has to be sabotaged in this case either.
